results.possibleIdentity: Richard Adler was a media scholar, researcher, and facilitator active in the late 1960s and 1970s, best known for his work at the intersection of media technology, art, and society. He served as the Associate Director and Program Officer of the Aspen Institute Program on Communications and Society in Palo Alto during the early 1970s. In this capacity, he edited and authored significant works exploring the cultural and social impact of television, such as "Television as a Cultural Force" (1976) and "Television as a Social Force" (1975). He was closely associated with the National Center for Experiments in Television (NCET), an organization founded in 1967 (and funded by the Ford Foundation) that paired artists with video engineers to explore the artistic potential of television—a mission very similar to that of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Adler is documented as having been an "Artist-in-Residence" at NCET and a participant in various conferences, such as "Television: Art and Information" (1973), which brought together artists, engineers, and scholars to discuss the emerging medium of video art and alternative television.

Richard Adler (1921–2012) was a prominent American composer, lyricist, and producer active in the 1960s and 1970s. While he is most famous for his 1950s Broadway hits "The Pajama Game" and "Damn Yankees," he remained a highly active figure in the arts during the specified period. In the early 1960s, he served as a White House Consultant for the Arts, staging and producing "Presidenticals" for the Kennedy and Johnson administrations, including the 1962 Madison Square Garden event where Marilyn Monroe sang "Happy Birthday" to President John F. Kennedy. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he transitioned into television work, composing the score for the 1967 TV musical "Olympus 7-0000" and producing several commercials that won industry awards. Later in his career (late 70s and 80s), he moved into symphonic and ballet compositions, such as the "Wilderness Suite" (1983), commissioned by the U.S. Department of the Interior. Although he was primarily a theatrical and musical artist, his role as a producer of large-scale programs and his work for television placed him at the center of mainstream American artistic production in the 1960s and 70s.

results.possibleIdentity: Norma-Jean Squires (also known as Norma Jean Squires) is a Southern California-based artist, painter, and sculptor who was active starting in the 1960s. She is an alumna of The Cooper Union (class of 1961) and has lived in Playa del Rey, California. Her work is primarily abstract and often utilizes media such as acrylic and interference on wood panel, Prismacolor, oilstick, and pastel on paper. In May 1975, she was featured in the "4x8+4x4" exhibition at the Newport Harbor Art Museum alongside other notable Southern California artists such as Lita Albuquerque and Martha Alf; at the time, she was associated with the Comsky Gallery in Los Angeles. Squires has been involved in the feminist art movement and the intersection of art and technology, participating in panels such as "Women Humanizing High Technology" at the Women's Caucus for Art (WCA) conference. Her artistic practice continued into the 2000s, with works like "Contratiempo" (2007) and "Triplicity" (2009) and participation in a 2008 traveling exhibition sponsored by the General Consulate of Mexico in Los Angeles.

results.possibleIdentity: Clifford Rose was an engineer associated with Bell Laboratories (Bell Labs) in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is identified in technical documentation as a member of the technical staff at Bell Labs, which was the primary source of engineering collaborators for the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Given E.A.T.'s mandate to foster collaborations between artists and engineers, he likely participated in the technical design or realization of art-technology projects. His name appears on lists of Bell Labs technical contributors during the same era as E.A.T.'s most active period (e.g., the NASA Synthesis Group report lists a Clifford Rose from Bell Labs).

Clifford Rose (1929–2021), born John Clifford Rose, was a prominent British actor active from the late 1950s through the 2010s. He was a founding member of the Royal Shakespeare Company (RSC) in 1960 and was later named an RSC Honorary Associate Artist. While primarily known for his work in theatre and television (including roles in the 1967 film adaptation of 'Marat/Sade' directed by Peter Brook, and as Sturmbannführer Ludwig Kessler in the series 'Secret Army'), his designation as an 'Associate Artist' and his career longevity align with the artist profile active in the late 1960s and 1970s. However, there is no widely documented direct link between him and the New York-based Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

results.possibleIdentity: Michael William "Mike" Hart (1943–2016) was a British singer-songwriter and poet who was a prominent figure in the Liverpool arts and music scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a founding member of the influential poetry-music collective The Liverpool Scene, alongside artist and poet Adrian Henri, Andy Roberts, and Mike Evans. The group was known for its experimental, intermedia performances that combined poetry, jazz, and rock, releasing albums such as 'The Incredible New Liverpool Scene' (1967). Hart later embarked on a solo career, recording the album 'Mike Hart Bleeds' (1969) for John Peel's Dandelion Records. His work is characterized by its gritty, beat-influenced lyrics and association with the UK underground arts movement.

Michael Stern Hart (1947–2011) was an American technologist and futurist, often called a "cyber-hippie," who is historically credited with inventing the electronic book. In 1971, while a student at the University of Illinois, he launched Project Gutenberg, the first digital library, by typing the U.S. Declaration of Independence into a mainframe computer. His work is frequently discussed in historical accounts of the intersection of art and technology, specifically following the legacy of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Some academic texts link his concept of "Replicator Technology"—the idea that digital items can be reproduced indefinitely—to the avant-garde movements of the 1960s that sought to merge artistic collaboration with automated labor and electronic media.

Michael J. Hart was an engineer and technical assistant associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He is specifically noted in historical records for his role as a technical staff member for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan—one of E.A.T.'s most ambitious projects. In this capacity, he worked alongside artists like Robert Breer and Frosty Myers to realize large-scale technological environments. While primarily recognized as an engineer, the collaborative nature of E.A.T. often blurred the lines between technical support and artistic creation during the late 1960s and early 1970s.
